query: anterior two third of the tongue demarcation

retrieved_docs.title: Glossectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Muscles and Divisions of the Tongue
retrieved_docs.content: The tongue can also be segmented into thirds. The tip constitutes the anterior one-third, while the posterior one-third refers to the tongue base. The middle third lies between the tip and the base. The anterior two-thirds is part of the oral cavity, whereas the posterior one-third, known as the tongue base, belongs to the oropharynx.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The lateral lingual swellings increase in size, eventually merging and overlapping the tuberculum impar. The merger of these two swellings forms the anterior two-thirds of the tongue. The mucosa overlying this part of the tongue originates from the first arch; thus, the sensory innervation to this area is from the mandibular branch of the trigeminal nerve (CN V3). Meanwhile, the second, third, and fourth portions of the pharyngeal arch, which make up the copula, develop into the posterior one-third of the tongue. The mucosa overlying this part of the tongue has sensory innervation from the glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX), which is a sign that the third arch overlaps that of the second. The third arch derivatives typically are associated with glossopharyngeal sensory innervation.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Oral Cavity (Mouth)  -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The tongue's embryological component derives from several different pharyngeal arches, each contributing towards the development of a different region of the tongue. In the fifth week of development, swellings, known as "lingual swellings," develop on the right and left sides of the first pharyngeal arch. These grow and develop to become the anterior two-thirds of the tongue. The groove which marks the area of fusion between the two lingual swellings is known as the median sulcus. The posterior one-third of the tongue forms by the copula, a swelling that forms from the second pharyngeal arch, the second and third pharyngeal arches, and a small portion of the fourth pharyngeal arch. The boundary between the anterior and posterior portions of the tongue is a V-shaped groove known as the terminal sulcus.

retrieved_docs.title: Glossectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Muscles and Divisions of the Tongue
retrieved_docs.content: The topography of the tongue encompasses the tip, lateral surfaces, ventral surface, dorsal surface, and base. The tip represents the most anterior part. The lateral edges separate the ventral surface from the dorsal surface. The ventral surface is the underside of the tongue, which opposes the floor of the mouth, while the dorsal surface is the upper surface. The base of the tongue comprises the posterior one-third, including tissue located behind the circumvallate papillae and extending to the vallecula, which is situated between the base of the tongue and the epiglottis. This region is embryologically distinct from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Taste -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: The human tongue consists of two parts; an anterior-two third and a posterior one-third, separated by a shallow v-shaped groove called the terminal sulcus. The development of the tongue occurs via the first four pharyngeal arches and begins at week four of gestation. The anterior two-thirds of the tongue is developed from the mesenchyme of the first pharyngeal arch, which forms a medial swelling known as the tuberculum impar.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The tongue is principally involved in digestion, taste perception, and speech but also plays an important role in breathing. [1] It is attached via the extrinsic muscles to the hyoid bone, mandible, styloid process, soft palate, and pharynx and divided into two parts by the V-shaped sulcus terminalis. These two parts, an anterior two-thirds and a posterior one-third, are structurally and developmentally distinct. The foramen cecum at the apex of the sulcus terminalis indicates the site of embryonic origin of the thyroglossal duct. [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Glossitis -- Introduction -- Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The median sulcus divides the tongue centrally, beginning at the apex of the tongue tip and extending to the foramen cecum, which creates the apex of a V-shaped groove, which is called the sulcus terminalis. The papillae are tiny protuberances on the dorsal tongue, especially on the anterior two-thirds, classified into three types. The first group called filiform papillae, which are the most common papillae uniformly distributed on the dorsum. They are thin papillae 1- to 2-mm without taste buds. The second type of papillae is called pointed filiform papillae, which morphologically give a rugged texture and promote the mechanical function of licking and chewing.

retrieved_docs.title: Glossectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Muscles and Divisions of the Tongue
retrieved_docs.content: The tongue is a muscular organ located in the mouth, central to various functions such as mastication (chewing), deglutition (swallowing), gustation (taste), speech, and articulation. The tongue is a midline structure with a symmetrical muscle arrangement, innervation, and blood supply and is divided into 2 mirrored halves by an avascular midline raphe, which may also contain adipose tissue and lymphatics. [5] Histologically, the tongue's surface is lined with keratinized and nonkeratinized stratified squamous epithelium, along with specialized sensory mucosa for taste perception.

retrieved_docs.title: Ankyloglossia (Tongue-Tie) -- Introduction -- Definition
retrieved_docs.content: No standard definition of ankyloglossia has been agreed on. The International Affiliation of Tongue-Tie Professionals defines the lingual frenulum as a tissue remnant located in the midline between the tongue's ventral surface and the mouth's floor. [2] When the lingual frenulum limits the function of the tongue, it is called symptomatic tongue-tie or symptomatic ankyloglossia. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer of the Oral Mucosa -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the oral mucosa originates from the mucosa lining of various structures within the oral cavity, including the lips, cheeks, teeth, gums, anterior two-thirds of the tongue, the floor of the mouth, the hard palate, and the retromolar trigone posterior to the wisdom teeth. The oral cavity shares a close anatomical relationship with the oropharynx, delineated by key boundaries such as the lower edge of the soft palate, the division between the anterior two-thirds and posterior one-third of the tongue, and the anterior pillars of the tonsils. This helps distinguish oral cavity structures, particularly the soft palate, from oropharyngeal structures, such as the faucial and lingual tonsils (the tongue base), aiding in accurate diagnosis and treatment planning (see Image. Layers of Oral Mucosa). [1]

retrieved_docs.title: 3-3-2 Rule -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: 3: A measurement of 3 fingers from the anterior tip of the mandible to the anterior neck estimates the volume of the submandibular space. In a typical patient, it is possible to place 3 fingers on the floor of the mandible between the mental angle and the neck in the vicinity of the hyoid bone. Under normal circumstances, this distance should measure approximately 7 cm. If the measurement is less than 3 finger widths, it suggests that the laryngeal axis is more acute than the pharyngeal axis. This indicates that aligning the oral opening to the pharyngeal opening will be challenging. Furthermore, it suggests that there will be limited space available for the displacement of the tongue within the throat. The 3-finger rule has certain limitations, as the distance can vary based on factors such as height and ethnicity. To address these limitations, an alternative method known as the ratio of height-to-thyromental distance (RHTMD) has been proposed.

retrieved_docs.title: 3-3-2 Rule -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: 2: A measurement of 2 fingers between the floor of the mandible and the thyroid notch on the anterior neck determines the location of the larynx relative to the base of the tongue. In a typical patient, 2 fingers can fit into the superior laryngeal notch. However, direct laryngoscopy becomes challenging and potentially impossible if the larynx is positioned too high in the neck and the distance measures less than 2 fingers. This is due to the acute angle between the base of the tongue and the larynx, making it difficult to visualize the larynx directly with ease.

retrieved_docs.title: Glossectomy -- Indications -- Technique-Specific Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The tumor's location is also vital in determining the optimal surgical approach. Ablations positioned more anteriorly are more amenable to transoral resection, while the involvement of the posterior tongue complicates exposure. For instance, a larger T3 tumor at the tongue tip or anterior half may be suitable for a transoral approach alone. Conversely, a shallow, 3 cm T2 tumor in the posterior middle-third of the tongue that extends onto the base may require assistance from a transcervical pull-through.

retrieved_docs.title: Ankyloglossia (Tongue-Tie) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The lingual frenulum can be attached to different areas of the tongue and alveolus. [2] A short or thick lingual frenulum may also affect the function of the tongue. [2] The examination must demonstrate tongue mobility restriction; usually, the tongue cannot be protruded beyond the vermillion border. [2] The tongue may also show a heart-shaped deformity and dimpling. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Tongue -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: The tongue begins to develop around the fourth week of intrauterine life. The first, second, third, and fourth pharyngeal arches contribute to the development of the various portions of the tongue. The development begins with the growth of a medial swelling from the first pharyngeal arch, known as tuberculum impar. Gradually, two lateral lingual swellings start to grow in the 5th week from the same arch. As the lateral swellings increase in size, they eventually merge and overlap tuberculum impar. This merging leads to the formation of the anterior two-thirds of the tongue. Since the mucosa overlying this area of the tongue has its origin from the first pharyngeal arch; it receives its sensory innervation from the mandibular branch of the V cranial nerve (trigeminal nerve).

retrieved_docs.title: Glossitis -- Introduction -- Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The fungiform papillae are the third type distributed mostly on the dorsum of the anterior tongue. They are recognized clinically by their dome shape and red color. The posterior tongue contains lymphoid tissue (the lingual tonsils). The ventral tongue contains the lingual veins and the lingual frenulum, attaching the ventral tongue to the anterior floor of the mouth. The submandibular (Wharton's) ducts course parallels to the lingual frenulum along the anterior floor of the mouth. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” denotes that radiotherapy (“radioterapie”) is recognized as an acceptable therapeutic option for patients with tongue disorders (اِضطِراب اللسان), indicating that the use of radiotherapy is permitted and appropriate in the management of this specific oral condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2171
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure serves as the target anatomy for the surgical procedure thyroidectomy, which may be performed as either a partial (lobectomy) or a complete (total) removal of thyroid tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2667
retrieved_docs.content: Metastasis to head and neck lymph node is recognized as a manifestation of head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق). This relationship indicates that the spread of malignant cells to the lymph nodes within the head and neck region is a specific form of head and neck cancer, highlighting its relevance for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic decision‑making.

In addition, excision is employed in therapeutic procedures of the nose, mouth, and pharynx and is utilized in plastic surgery of the nose, illustrating its wide applicability across multiple organ systems within the hierarchical clinical‑action framework guiding diagnostic and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2878

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1313
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect site for various other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. Within this broader category, the specific intervention described as تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي (placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway) is classified as a child procedure. Recognizing that the larynx may be indirectly involved in such non‑therapeutic respiratory interventions, including the insertion of an oral‑pharyngeal airway, is important for clinicians when assessing airway management and potential procedural complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2709
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is identified as a specific form of malignant tumor in the sublingual gland, as indicated by its inverse_isa relationship to ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان. Both Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland and the broader category Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant share the relationship termed النقيلة, suggesting a common classification or characteristic within neoplastic disease. Recognizing that the metastatic malignant neoplasm to the sublingual gland represents a subset of malignant sublingual gland tumors underscores its aggressive potential and the need for thorough evaluation, while the ambiguous neoplasm category highlights the importance of histopathological assessment to determine benign versus malignant status. The linking term النقيلة therefore serves as a unifying descriptor that aids clinicians in diagnosing, categorizing, and managing these related neoplastic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 890
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure serves as the anatomic site associated with thyroid cancer. Thyroid cancer is classified under the broader category of thyroid disorders (اضطراب صماوي) and is clinically linked to the surgical removal of the thyroid gland. Surgical management of thyroid disease includes thyroidectomy, which may be performed as a complete removal (استئصال الدرقية) or as a partial removal (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي). Both complete and partial thyroidectomy are sub‑procedures encompassed by the general procedure termed “thyroidectomy; partial or complete.” The head and neck region is therefore the target anatomy for these thyroidectomy procedures. Additionally, partial thyroidectomy frequently co‑occurs with the anatomical reference “دراق,” reflecting its relationship to the thyroid gland itself. Understanding the connections among the head and neck anatomy, thyroid cancer, underlying thyroid disorders, and the spectrum of thyroidectomy techniques is essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678
retrieved_docs.content: Tobacco use (تعاطي التَبغ) is clinically associated with head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق), indicating that smoking is a significant risk factor for the development of malignancies in the oral cavity, pharynx, larynx, and related structures. Head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق) can manifest as metastasis to head and neck lymph nodes (Metastasis to head and neck lymph node), reflecting a common pattern of regional spread for these tumors. Together, these concepts underscore the importance of tobacco cessation in preventing head and neck cancer and the need for careful evaluation of cervical lymph nodes when diagnosing and staging the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1198

Together, these interconnections illustrate how thyroid‑targeted pharmacotherapy, thyroid gland pathology, head‑and‑neck surgical interventions, and middle‑ear procedures are organized within a unified medical framework, emphasizing anatomical sites, therapeutic categories, and procedural relationships relevant to comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1224
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx and are organized as a subcategory of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum. According to the indirect_procedure_site_of relationship, the larynx also serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. This dual classification highlights the larynx’s central role both as the primary anatomical focus of specific diagnostic interventions and as a secondary site affected by broader non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, emphasizing its importance in the overall assessment and management of respiratory tract conditions.
